active_projects.description: Design opencode.json agent registration mechanism for the extension system including manifest changes, merge/unmerge strategy, and validation

artifacts.summary: Design report for opencode.json agent registration mechanism covering manifest changes, merge/unmerge strategy, validation pipeline, conflict resolution, sync lifecycle, and managed/unmanaged distinction
Implementation plan with 3 phases documenting design decisions, creating reference docs, and adding code annotations
Implementation summary documenting 5 design decisions, 4 reference documents, and code annotations across 5 Lua files

memory_candidates.content: The extension system uses a managed/unmanaged sidecar file pattern (.opencode.json.managed) to distinguish automatically-managed config files from user-customized ones. This pattern should be extended to govern sync overwrite behavior and cleanup scope, not just base template installation.

The key-based tracking pattern for JSON merge/unmerge (storing added keys in state, removing only tracked keys on unmerge) is a reusable design for any JSON file that needs idempotent extension merging. It preserves user additions and handles missing files gracefully.

Agent name conflicts across extensions are currently handled silently (first-loaded wins, second is skipped). The recommended design is to add explicit conflict detection with user warnings, and document that agent names should be unique across the extension ecosystem.

The OpenCode extension system uses a key-based JSON merge tracking pattern for idempotent unmerge: merge tracks added keys in {keys = {...}}, unmerge removes only tracked keys, and state persists across sessions in extensions.json. This pattern is reusable for any JSON file that needs merge/unmerge semantics and could be generalized beyond opencode.json to other config files.

The TODO(541) annotation pattern in Lua files references the design spec path explicitly, making it easy for future agents to find the relevant design decisions. This pattern can be reused for any design task that produces implementation-guiding annotations.

The JSON merge tracking pattern (key-based tracking with {keys = {...}}) is a reusable design for any additive, reversible JSON merging. It was documented in .opencode/context/patterns/json-merge-tracking.md with pseudocode and reusability instructions.

active_projects.completion_summary: Formalized 5 design decisions into an authoritative design specification, created 4 reusable reference documents for the extension ecosystem, and added structured TODO/FIXME/NOTE annotations to 5 key Lua files to guide future implementation agents.

active_projects.description: Research opencode.json schema, CLI validation behavior, and gaps in extension agent registration that cause startup crashes

artifacts.summary: Research on opencode.json schema, CLI validation, and extension agent registration gaps
Implementation plan with 5 phases to fix automatic agent registration in opencode.json
Implementation summary for automatic opencode.json agent registration fix

memory_candidates.content: The OpenCode extension loader has fully-implemented merge/unmerge code for opencode.json agent registration (merge_opencode_agents/unmerge_opencode_agents in merge.lua), but ZERO of 17 extension manifests declare merge_targets.opencode_json, making the code path unreachable. This is why extension agents are invisible to the CLI and why manual opencode.json edits cause crashes on unload.

The opencode.json base template at .opencode/templates/opencode.json is never synced to project root because sync.lua excludes it from root files. This means projects start without a valid opencode.json unless one is created manually, contributing to the manual-edit crash pattern.

Pre-merge validation pattern for opencode.json: before writing agent entries, verify all {file:...} references resolve to existing files. The loader copies agent .md files before processing merge targets, so by the time merge_opencode_agents runs, the referenced files should exist. This validation prevents malformed extension manifests from corrupting project opencode.json.

active_projects.completion_summary: Fixed OpenCode extension loader automatic agent registration by adding pre-merge validation, creating 14 opencode-agents.json fragments, updating 14 extension manifests with merge_targets.opencode_json, syncing base opencode.json template, and adding startup cleanup for stale references.

active_projects.description: Investigate and implement context: fork + agent: frontmatter for core delegating skills to enable CLAUDE_CODE_FORK_SUBAGENT=1 cache sharing, following corrected understanding of fork mechanics from official Claude Code docs

artifacts.summary: Revised plan to investigate and implement context: fork + agent: frontmatter for core delegating skills, enabling FORK_SUBAGENT cache sharing with 4 phases: audit, pilot, rollout, documentation

Definitive architecture analysis: hybrid inline-agent approach has no precedent, context: fork + orchestration IS proven (slide skills), cache sharing is fundamentally incompatible with named agent routing, recommend rescoping task 500 to documentation-only

memory_candidates.content: All loaded extension skills (neovim-research, neovim-implementation, nix-research, nix-implementation) use the same Pattern A delegation as core skills -- explicit Task tool with subagent_type and multi-stage postflight. They do NOT use context: fork or agent: frontmatter. Only unloaded extension skills (e.g., slide-planning, slide-critic) use Pattern B with context: fork.

The present extension's skill-slide-planning and skill-slide-critic skills use context: fork + agent: in frontmatter AND explicit subagent_type in their Task call. This proves that context: fork with multi-stage orchestration (preflight, interactive Q&A, delegation, postflight) works correctly.
Claude Code agent definitions support a skills field that preloads skill content into the subagent context at startup. This is described as the inverse of context: fork.

active_projects.description: Optimize team-mode skills (team-research, team-plan, team-implement) to maximize FORK_SUBAGENT parallel cache sharing benefits for teammates 2-N

artifacts.summary: Team-mode skills are already eligible for FORK_SUBAGENT cache sharing; optimization focuses on prompt restructuring, dynamic token multiplier calculation, and cache-aware metadata reporting.
4-phase plan to restructure team-mode prompts for FORK_SUBAGENT cache sharing, replace hardcoded token multiplier with dynamic formula, and update documentation

memory_candidates.content: TeammateTool spawns in team-mode skills do NOT specify subagent_type, making them eligible for CLAUDE_CODE_FORK_SUBAGENT=1 cache sharing. Teammates 2-N inherit the parent's prompt cache prefix, reducing input token cost by ~90%. Both CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS=1 and CLAUDE_CODE_FORK_SUBAGENT=1 must be enabled. The actual cache benefit depends on the byte-identical shared prefix ratio -- prompts should put shared content first and teammate-specific assignments last to maximize overlap.

skill-team-research hardcodes team_size=4 in Stage 1 regardless of user-provided --team-size flag. The four fixed roles are: Primary (A), Alternatives (B), Critic (C), Horizons (D). The token_usage_multiplier is hardcoded at 5.0 in all team skill metadata but should be dynamically calculated.
